title: practical english usage - Part 88
content:
You seem very sure of passing the exam. I hope you're right.

realised it wasn't going to be so easy.

Before the game she felt certain of winning, but after a few minutes she talking about.

Certain!sure of + -ing are used to refer to the feelings of the person one is 15  certain        and   sure Sorry to keep you waiting - we can start now.

I'm sorry to tell you that you failed the exam.

Sorry to disturb you - could I speak to you for a moment?

doing or going to do, or that one has just done.

Sorry + infinitive is used to apologise for current situations - things that one is or I'm sorry to have woken you up. (   I'm sorry that I woke you up.) Sorry + perfect infinitive (more formal) can be used with the same meaning.

I'm sorry for/about losing my temper this morning, (   I'm sorry that I lost (ThaZ-clauses are also very common in an informal style.) Sorry for! about + -ing is used to refer to past things that one regrets.

I'm not afraid of telling/ to tell her the truth.

difference of meaning.

ɪn other cases we  can use afraid of + -ing or afraid + infinitive with no 'Why are you so quiet? 'I'm afraid of waking the children!

I don't like to drive fast because I'm afraid of crashing.

To  talk about fear of things that happen accidentally, we  prefer afraid of + -ing.

I stopped to rest. (= ... in order to rest.) not I stopped running, (     .. . I stopped to run!) of purpose (►   112). A  common example is stop. Compare: Some verbs that are followed by -ing forms can also be followed by an infinitive 12 -ing    form    or  infinitive       of  purpose:     stop -ing                                                         Section9 Infinitives,        forms     and   Past   Participles       after    Nouns,    Verbs,   etc grammar •  106   active and passive infinitive with similar meaning For more about be + infinitive, ►    42.

For structures with take (e.g.  The ferry took two hours to unload). ►    602.

For structures like She’s easy to amuse, ►     ] 01 .4.

of putting it right.) There's nothing to be done - we'll have to buy a new one. (= There's no way I'm bored - there's nothing to do. (= There are no entertainments.) Note the difference between nothing to do and nothing to be done.

3 nothing         to   do   and   nothing         to   be   done,      etc Nobody was to blame for the accident.

unfortunate event).

But note the common expression to blame, meaning 'responsible' (for some This behaviour is to be encouraged.

You are to be congratulated, (     .. ,-to-eongratulate/) congratulate, encourage, avoid.

We   also use passive infinitives to express value judgements with verbs like not Susan was nowhere to be found, (     ... nowhere to find;) not He wasn't anywhere to be seen, (     ... anywhere-to see.) Note the expressions anywhere/ nowhere to be seen/found.

2  to  be   seen/found/congratulated,                         etc not The cleaning is to be finished by midday, (     .. .-is to finish ...) not This form is to be filled in in ink. (     This form is to filbin ...) not These sheets are to be washed, (     These sheets are to wash;) After be, we  normally use passive infinitives.

His desk is covered with forms to be filled in.

The carpets to be cleaned are in the garage, (     The-carpets to-elean-...) person/thing that the action is done to.

We   use passive infinitives if we  are thinking more about the action, or the They've sent Megan a form to complete.

I've got work to do. (     P&e-got-work-to-be done.) the action.

We   prefer active infinitives if we  are thinking more about the person who  will do The people to interview / to be interviewed are in the next room.

Give me the names of the people to contact / to be contacted.

There's a lot of work to do / to be done.

to do. Active and passive infinitives are often both possible.

We   can use noun + infinitive to talk about obligation - things that people have 1  obligation similar     meaning 106 active     and   passive      infinitive       with sot help me? (     I'm interested to w&rkdn-SwiteeflaHd-...) I'm interested in working in Switzerland. Do you know anybody who could To  talk about a wish to do something, we  use interested in with an -ing form.
